name: Theranos
summary: Blood-testing startup claimed its Edison device could run hundreds of tests from a finger-prick, but the technology never worked and results were faked or run on standard lab equipment.
lesson: Regulatory and clinical claims cannot be faked indefinitely once real patients and inspectors are involved.

name: Dendreon
summary: Developer of Provenge, the first FDA-approved cancer immunotherapy vaccine, collapsed into Chapter 11 bankruptcy because manufacturing costs and a $93,000 price tag made commercial uptake unsustainable.
lesson: FDA approval is not commercial viability if the cost structure cannot support market adoption.

name: Achaogen
summary: Won FDA approval for antibiotic Zemdri in 2018 but filed for bankruptcy within a year because hospitals rarely used novel antibiotics, generating almost no revenue.
lesson: Antibiotics face a broken market where clinical need doesn't translate into reimbursed volume.

name: Melinta Therapeutics
summary: Antibiotic maker with four approved drugs filed Chapter 11 in 2020 after revenue failed to cover debt and operating costs, the second antibiotic bankruptcy in a year.
lesson: Multiple approved antibiotics still couldn't overcome the structurally poor economics of the antibacterial market.

name: Axovant Sciences
summary: Bought Alzheimer's candidate intepirdine from GSK; the drug failed its phase 3 trial in 2017, and the company wound down its neuroscience pipeline and rebranded.
lesson: Licensing a deprioritized asset from a major pharma is often a signal, not a bargain.

name: Insys Therapeutics
summary: Maker of fentanyl spray Subsys collapsed into bankruptcy after founder John Kapoor and executives were convicted of bribing doctors to overprescribe the powerful opioid.
lesson: Aggressive off-label sales tactics for a controlled substance invite criminal liability that can end a company overnight.

name: Clovis Oncology
summary: Developer of PARP inhibitor Rubraca filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after the approved ovarian cancer drug underperformed commercially against competitors.
lesson: Being second-to-market in oncology against an entrenched competitor drug can doom even an approved therapy.

name: Genocea Biosciences
summary: T-cell-directed herpes vaccine and later cancer immunotherapy programs failed to show sufficient efficacy, leading to bankruptcy after burning through hundreds of millions.
lesson: A differentiated immunology platform still needs a clinical winner within a finite cash runway.

name: Human Longevity Inc
summary: Craig Venter's ambitious longevity and whole-genome sequencing company faced co-founder lawsuits, leadership turnover, and business-model struggles, eventually selling off major assets.
lesson: A visionary founding team is not sufficient without a validated near-term revenue model in genomics.

name: deCODE genetics
summary: Icelandic population-genomics pioneer that mapped disease genes filed for bankruptcy after its drug discovery efforts failed to generate products.
lesson: A world-class genomics dataset does not automatically convert into a marketable drug pipeline.

name: 23andMe
summary: Consumer genomics company shut down its therapeutics division after failing to produce a viable pipeline, then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid a data breach scandal.
lesson: A massive proprietary genetic dataset is a research asset, not automatically a drug discovery business.

name: Juno Therapeutics
summary: CAR-T therapy JCAR015 caused five patient deaths from cerebral edema, forcing an FDA clinical hold before the company was acquired by Celgene in 2018.
lesson: Fatal neurotoxicity in a CAR-T trial can end a flagship program even amid an otherwise hot cell-therapy market.

name: bluebird bio
summary: Gene therapy pioneer won multiple approvals but pricing them at $2.8-3.1M each proved commercially unsustainable, forcing withdrawal from Europe and an 80%+ stock decline.
lesson: Curative one-time gene therapies still need a reimbursement pathway that health systems will actually pay for.

name: CureVac
summary: Early mRNA vaccine pioneer's COVID-19 candidate showed only ~47% efficacy, losing the vaccine race to Moderna and Pfizer/BioNTech despite two decades of platform development.
lesson: Being first to a technology platform does not guarantee being first with a winning clinical product.

name: Inovio Pharmaceuticals
summary: DNA vaccine developer's COVID-19 candidate was repeatedly delayed by FDA holds and its device-based delivery approach, missing the vaccine race entirely.
lesson: A vaccine delivery technology that requires specialized hardware faces a much slower regulatory and manufacturing path.

name: Sangamo Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of zinc-finger gene editing has seen a string of discontinued programs and multiple partner exits despite three decades of platform development.
lesson: An early technological lead in gene editing erodes quickly once CRISPR-based competitors demonstrate cleaner clinical data.

name: Biogen (Aduhelm)
summary: Alzheimer's drug Aduhelm received a controversial accelerated FDA approval over its own advisory committee's objections, then was rejected by insurers and withdrawn from market in 2024.
lesson: An approval secured against expert consensus does not guarantee physician adoption or payer reimbursement.

name: Cassava Sciences
summary: Alzheimer's drug developer faced sustained allegations of manipulated preclinical data behind lead candidate simufilam, triggering FDA and DOJ investigations and a stock collapse.
lesson: Data integrity questions raised by independent scientists can unravel years of investor confidence even before a pivotal trial reads out.

name: MannKind Corporation
summary: Inhaled insulin product Afrezza faced multiple near-bankruptcies before winning approval, but adoption remained tiny for years against injectable insulin's entrenched market position.
lesson: A better delivery mechanism doesn't overcome physician and patient inertia around an established standard of care.

name: Editas Medicine
summary: One of the original CRISPR gene-editing companies discontinued its lead in vivo program after disappointing trial results, forcing a strategic pivot and repeated leadership changes.
lesson: Being a CRISPR pioneer with strong IP does not guarantee the first clinical program succeeds.

name: Moderna
summary: mRNA vaccine platform company developed Spikevax, one of the first authorized COVID-19 vaccines, generating tens of billions in revenue and validating mRNA as a viable drug modality.
lesson: A platform technology with no approved products can become one of the fastest-scaling drug companies in history when the right crisis and science align.

name: BioNTech
summary: German mRNA biotech partnered with Pfizer to develop the first FDA-authorized COVID-19 vaccine, transforming from an obscure oncology-focused startup into a global vaccine powerhouse.
lesson: Partnering with a large pharma company for manufacturing and distribution can convert deep platform science into global-scale impact almost immediately.

name: Regeneron Pharmaceuticals
summary: Built a durable pipeline of blockbuster biologics including Eylea and Dupixent using its proprietary antibody-discovery platform, becoming one of the most consistently profitable biotechs.
lesson: A reusable antibody-discovery engine that generates multiple blockbusters outcompetes single-asset biotech models over decades.

name: Vertex Pharmaceuticals
summary: Dominated the cystic fibrosis drug market with a sequence of increasingly effective CFTR modulators, and co-developed the first CRISPR-based approved therapy with CRISPR Therapeutics.
lesson: Owning an entire disease category through iterative drug improvement builds a far more durable moat than one-off blockbusters.

name: Alnylam Pharmaceuticals
summary: Pioneered RNA interference as a drug modality, winning FDA approval for the first-ever RNAi therapeutic in 2018, followed by several more approved RNAi drugs.
lesson: Persisting through nearly two decades of platform validation risk can create an entirely new drug modality and category leadership.

name: Seagen
summary: Pioneered antibody-drug conjugate technology and built a portfolio of approved cancer drugs, culminating in Pfizer's $43 billion acquisition in 2023.
lesson: Owning a foundational linker-payload technology for an entire therapeutic class compounds value across many downstream drugs.

name: Kite Pharma
summary: Developed Yescarta, one of the first FDA-approved CAR-T cell therapies for lymphoma, and was acquired by Gilead for nearly $12 billion just three years after its IPO.
lesson: Being first to market with a genuinely curative cell therapy in an underserved cancer indication commands enormous acquisition premiums.

name: CRISPR Therapeutics
summary: Co-developed Casgevy with Vertex, the first CRISPR gene-editing therapy ever approved by the FDA (2023), for sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia.
lesson: Founding a company around a Nobel Prize-winning technology can deliver the first approved product in that class within a decade.

name: Karuna Therapeutics
funding: Acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb for $14B
summary: Developed the first new mechanism-of-action schizophrenia drug in decades, avoiding dopamine-blocking side effects of older antipsychotics, and was acquired for $14 billion.
lesson: A genuinely novel mechanism in a therapeutic area starved of innovation for 70 years commands enormous strategic value.

name: BioMarin Pharmaceutical
summary: Built a durable franchise of rare-disease enzyme-replacement and gene therapies, becoming one of the most consistently successful rare-disease drug developers over more than two decades.
lesson: Focusing exclusively on ultra-rare diseases with little competition builds a durable, high-margin franchise that larger pharma companies avoid.
